questions.helper: This answer is optional and does not select your legal pathway. The backend uses the factual answers and state rules.

questions.prompt: Ask in this order:
Was this a Vermont case, federal case, or out-of-state case?
Was there a conviction, or was it dismissed, acquitted, no probable cause, or never charged?
What county and court handled it?
What is the docket number?
What was the exact offense and statute?
Were all charges from the same incident?
How old were you when the offense happened?
When did you complete every part of the sentence, including probation, supervision, jail, fines, restitution, and surcharges?
Do you owe restitution, surcharges, public defender fees, or court-ordered payments?
Do you have any pending criminal charges?
Have you had any later convictions?
Was the offense a DUI?
Do you have a commercial drivers license or permit?
Was the conviction for conduct that is no longer a crime, like certain cannabis conduct?
Was the offense a listed crime, sex offense, domestic/protective-order offense, vulnerable-adult offense, child offense, animal-cruelty offense, hate-motivated offense, or registry-related offense?
Would the prosecutor stipulate?
How old were you when the offense or case began?
Have you completed every part of the sentence, supervision, probation, parole, classes, or community service?
Are all fines, restitution, court costs, and other required financial obligations resolved?
Do you currently have any pending criminal charge, open case, probation, parole, or other supervision?
Have you received a pardon, certificate of rehabilitation, or similar executive relief for this conviction?
Was the arrest or record caused by mistaken identity, identity theft, or law-enforcement error?
When was the case disposed of, dismissed, completed, or discharged?
Which court or agency handled the case?
What charge, statute, or record description appears on the record?
Do you have court paperwork or a criminal-history record showing the exact charge and outcome?
Where in Vermont was the case handled?
What case, docket, cause, or arrest number appears on the record?

pathways.label: Adult conviction expungement — narrow statutory route
pathways.summary: Under current 13 V.S.A. 7602, a person may petition to expunge a conviction record only if the person was convicted of an offense where the underlying conduct is no longer prohibited by law or no longer designated as a criminal offense. The court grants if the person completed sentence/supervision and paid restitution and surcharges, unless surcharges were waived. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/section/13/230/07602") Common examples include old cannabis/marijuana conduct that is no longer criminal, but Wilma must verify the exact statute, amount, conviction offense, and current law before saying it fits. is not enough. For delinquency matters adjudicated on or after July 1, 1996, Vermont generally seals the record after 2 years from final discharge unless the States Attorney objects and the court finds a listed-crime issue or lack of rehabilitation. For older delinquency matters, sealing is available by application or court motion if there is no later listed-crime conviction/adjudication or pending proceeding, and rehabilitation is shown. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/section/33/051/05119?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

For dismissed juvenile matters, if the person was charged with a criminal offense or was subject to a delinquency petition on or after July 1, 2006 and before age of majority, the court records are sealed immediately when the case is dismissed. Older dismissed juvenile cases may be sealed after 2 years from dismissal. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/section/33/051/05119?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")

Wilma rule: If the misdemeanor involves domestic violence, stalking, sex conduct, child/vulnerable adult abuse, protection orders, animal cruelty, hate crime, or sex-offender registration, route to manual review.

Qualifying felonies include certain burglary, designated felony property offenses, certain regulated-drug offenses, and any offense for which the person received an unconditional pardon from the Governor. The property-offense list includes forgery/counterfeiting, false pretenses, home-improvement fraud, identity theft, grand larceny, embezzlement, receiving stolen property, retail theft, theft of services, failure to return rented property, false claims, unlawful mischief, unlawful trespass, certain mills/dams/bridges offenses, unauthorized removal of human remains, grave-marker offenses, computer crimes, and regulated-drug fraud/deceit. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")
Wilma rule: Felony sealing in Vermont is statute-specific. Do not route a felony as eligible unless the exact statute is on the qualifying list or the person has an unconditional pardon.

There is generally no filing fee to ask to seal or expunge a Vermont criminal record. The exception is sealing a DUI conviction under 23 V.S.A. 1201(a) committed when the person was 25 or older; the Vermont Judiciary says there is a filing fee for that route, and a person can request waiver using Application to Waive Filing Fees and Service Costs - 600-00229. ("https://www.vtcourts.gov/criminal/expungement")

pathways.label: Non-conviction sealing
pathways.summary: Non-conviction records are now broadly sealable. The court should issue a sealing order within 60 days after final disposition, unless a party objects in the interests of justice, if: the court did not find probable cause at arraignment; the charge was dismissed before trial, with or without prejudice; or the defendant was acquitted. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")
ruleClauses: Non-conviction records are now broadly sealable.
The court should issue a sealing order within 60 days after final disposition, unless a party objects in the interests of justice, if:
the court did not find probable cause at arraignment;
the charge was dismissed before trial, with or without prejudice; or
the defendant was acquitted. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")

A person may also file a petition at any time, and the court grants if sealing serves the interests of justice or if the parties stipulate. If either party objects, the court schedules a hearing, and only the defendant and prosecutor are parties. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")
Wilma rule: Dismissed, no-probable-cause, and acquittal records are sealing-first. Do not promise expungement unless a separate expungement route applies.

Vermont post-2025 = sealing-first, expungement-narrow.Expungement is mainly for convictions where the conduct is no longer criminal.Misdemeanor sealing = 3 years after completion, unless excluded.Felony sealing = 7 years after completion, but only listed felony categories.DUI sealing = 10 years, no CDL, special fee if offense committed at 25 or older.Non-convictions should seal within 60 days unless objection, and can be petitioned later.Age matters: 18-21 has a 30-day qualifying-crime route; under 25 has a broader 2-year rehabilitation route.Do not promise relief until Wilma has the docket, offense statute, disposition, sentence-completion date, payment status, pending-charge status, age at offense, and prosecutor-stipulation status.

For adult felony sealing exact felony is on Vermonts qualifying list or unconditional pardon applies; 7 years have passed since sentence completion; restitution/surcharges are paid or waived; no pending charge blocks court action.
For DUI sealing 10 years have passed since sentence completion; no CDL/commercial permit; restitution/surcharges paid or waived; if offense was before age 25, under-25 route checked.

For under-25 sealing offense date before 25th birthday; 2 years since final discharge; no listed-crime conviction/adjudication in prior 10 years; no pending listed-crime proceeding; rehabilitation evidence available.

Vermont post-2025 = sealing-first, expungement-narrow.Expungement is mainly for convictions where the conduct is no longer criminal.Misdemeanor sealing = 3 years after completion, unless excluded.Felony sealing = 7 years after completion, but only listed felony categories.DUI sealing = 10 years, no CDL, special fee if offense committed at 25 or older.Non-convictions should seal within 60 days unless objection, and can be petitioned later.Age matters: 18-21 has a 30-day qualifying-crime route; under 25 has a broader 2-year rehabilitation route.Do not promise relief until Wilma has the docket, offense statute, disposition, sentence-completion date, payment status, pending-charge status, age at offense, and prosecutor-stipulation status.

exclusionRules.ruleText: If the person has a pending criminal charge when the sealing/expungement petition is before the court, the court cannot act until the new charge is resolved. If a petition is denied, the person generally must wait at least 2 years before filing another petition unless the court authorizes a shorter period. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")
Wilma rule: Always ask about pending charges anywhere in Vermont before saying the petition is ready.
If the person has a pending criminal charge when the sealing/expungement petition is before the court, the court cannot act until the new charge is resolved.
Most Vermont misdemeanor convictions are sealable unless excluded by the

Under 13 V.S.A. 7609, a person who was 18-21 when they committed a qualifying crime may petition to seal after 30 days have passed since completing the sentence. Restitution and surcharges must be paid or waived, and the prosecutor must not show sealing would be contrary to the interests of justice. A record containing both qualifying and nonqualifying offenses is not eligible under this section. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")

sourceSections.text:
Users case outcome First Wilma route Vermont treatment
Conviction for conduct that is no longer a crime Expungement Possible after sentence/supervision complete and restitution/surcharges paid
Most eligible misdemeanor convictions Sealing 3 years after sentence completion
Eligible felony property/drug/burglary/pardon categories Sealing 7 years after sentence completion
Qualifying DUI misdemeanor Sealing 10 years after sentence completion; CDL holders barred
No probable cause at arraignment Sealing Court should seal within 60 days unless a party objects
Dismissed before trial Sealing Court should seal within 60 days unless a party objects
Acquittal Sealing Court should seal within 60 days unless a party objects
Prosecutor and defendant stipulate Sealing or expungement depending on route Court may grant without hearing
Offense committed age 18-21 Special sealing route Qualifying crime may be sealed 30 days after sentence completion
Offense committed before age 25 Young-person sealing route 2 years after final discharge, no listed-crime conviction/adjudication in past 10 years, no pending listed-crime proceeding, rehabilitation shown
Juvenile delinquency Juvenile sealing Generally 2 years after final discharge unless State objects and court finds listed-crime issue or lack of rehabilitation
Governor unconditional pardon Sealing route Treated as qualifying crime under current 7601; do not promise expungement unless another expungement basis exists

sourceSections.title: Remedy definitions Wilma must understand
sourceSections.text:
Expungement
Vermont expungement annuls the record of arrest, conviction, and sentence, and the person is treated as if they were never arrested, convicted, or sentenced for the offense. The response to public inquiry must be
NO CRIMINAL RECORD EXISTS.
The court removes the expunged offense from accessible databases, and when all charges on a docket are expunged, the case file is destroyed under Court Administrator policies. (

"https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")
Sealing
A sealed record is not publicly viewable, but it is not destroyed. The person is still generally treated as if they were never arrested, convicted, or sentenced, and the public response is also
NO CRIMINAL RECORD EXISTS.
But sealed records remain available for specific exceptions, including criminal justice purposes, firearm-background-check purposes, discovery obligations, law-enforcement employment/internal investigation, and certain litigation uses. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")
Wilma rule: In Vermont, do not call ordinary conviction relief
expungement
after the 2025 change. For most convictions, say:
This looks like a possible sealing route, not expungement.

sourceSections.title: Adult conviction expungement - now narrow
sourceSections.text:
Under current 13 V.S.A. 7602, a person may petition to expunge a conviction record only if the person was convicted of an offense where the underlying conduct is no longer prohibited by law or no longer designated as a criminal offense. The court grants if the person completed sentence/supervision and paid restitution and surcharges, unless surcharges were waived. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/section/13/230/07602")
Common examples include old cannabis/marijuana conduct that is no longer criminal, but Wilma must verify the exact statute, amount, conviction offense, and current law before saying it fits.
Wilma rule:
Old marijuana
is not enough. Ask what the person was actually convicted of possessing/selling/cultivating, the amount, the statute, and the date.

"https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/section/13/230/07602")
Misdemeanor exclusions / danger zones
Vermonts qualifying-crime definition excludes several misdemeanor categories, including listed crimes, child sexual exploitation, abuse-prevention/stalking/sexual-assault/protective-order violations, vulnerable-adult abuse/exploitation, voyeurism, animal cruelty, sex-offender-registry failure, hate-motivated crimes, cruelty to a child, mistreatment of persons with impaired cognitive function, female genital mutilation, sexual exploitation of a minor, extreme-risk-protection-order violation, CDL motor-vehicle offenses, and any offense requiring sex-offender registration. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")
Wilma rule: If the misdemeanor involves domestic violence, stalking, sex conduct, child/vulnerable adult abuse, protection orders, animal cruelty, hate crime, or sex-offender registration, route to manual review.

sourceSections.title: Adult conviction sealing - felonies
sourceSections.text:
Vermont felony sealing is limited to the felony categories listed in 13 V.S.A. 7601(4)(B).
Basic felony sealing rule

Requirement Rule
Offense type Qualifying felony
Waiting period 7 years after completion of sentence terms and conditions
Restitution/surcharges Must be paid, unless surcharge waived
Prosecutor burden Prosecutor must not show sealing is contrary to the interests of justice
These conditions come from 13 V.S.A. 7602(d). (

"https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/section/13/230/07602")
Qualifying felony categories
Qualifying felonies include certain burglary, designated felony property offenses, certain regulated-drug offenses, and any offense for which the person received an unconditional pardon from the Governor. The property-offense list includes forgery/counterfeiting, false pretenses, home-improvement fraud, identity theft, grand larceny, embezzlement, receiving stolen property, retail theft, theft of services, failure to return rented property, false claims, unlawful mischief, unlawful trespass, certain mills/dams/bridges offenses, unauthorized removal of human remains, grave-marker offenses, computer crimes, and regulated-drug fraud/deceit. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")
Wilma rule: Felony sealing in Vermont is statute-specific. Do not route a felony as eligible unless the exact statute is on the qualifying list or the person has an unconditional pardon.

sourceSections.title: DUI sealing
sourceSections.text:
A qualifying DUI misdemeanor can be sealed if:

Requirement Rule
Waiting period 10 years after sentence completion
Restitution/surcharges Paid or waived as applicable
CDL status Person is not a commercial drivers license or commercial permit holder
Prosecutor burden Prosecutor does not show sealing is contrary to interests of justice
This rule is in 13 V.S.A. 7602(e). (

"https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/section/13/230/07602")
Wilma rule: For DUI, always ask:
Were you 25 or older when the DUI happened?
and
Do you have a CDL or commercial learners permit?
DUI committed under 25 may have a different young-person sealing path under 33 V.S.A. 5119.

sourceSections.title: Non-conviction sealing
sourceSections.text:
Non-conviction records are now broadly sealable.
The court should issue a sealing order within 60 days after final disposition, unless a party objects in the interests of justice, if:
the court did not find probable cause at arraignment;
the charge was dismissed before trial, with or without prejudice; or
the defendant was acquitted. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")
A person may also file a petition at any time, and the court grants if sealing serves the interests of justice or if the parties stipulate. If either party objects, the court schedules a hearing, and only the defendant and prosecutor are parties. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")
Wilma rule: Dismissed, no-probable-cause, and acquittal records are sealing-first. Do not promise expungement unless a separate expungement route applies.

sourceSections.title: Pending charges and denial rule
sourceSections.text:
If the person has a pending criminal charge when the sealing/expungement petition is before the court, the court cannot act until the new charge is resolved. If a petition is denied, the person generally must wait at least 2 years before filing another petition unless the court authorizes a shorter period. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")
Wilma rule: Always ask about pending charges anywhere in Vermont before saying the petition is ready.

sourceSections.title: Young adult sealing - age 18-21
sourceSections.text:
Under 13 V.S.A. 7609, a person who was 18-21 when they committed a qualifying crime may petition to seal after 30 days have passed since completing the sentence. Restitution and surcharges must be paid or waived, and the prosecutor must not show sealing would be contrary to the interests of justice. A record containing both qualifying and nonqualifying offenses is not eligible under this section. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/fullchapter/13/230")
Wilma rule: This is a fast sealing path, but only for qualifying crimes. If one docket has both qualifying and nonqualifying charges, flag manual review.
11. Offenses committed before age 25 - 33 V.S.A. 5119(g)
Vermont has a broader young-person sealing route for a person who pleaded guilty to or was convicted of a Vermont crime committed before age 25. The court shall seal if:

Requirement Rule
Waiting period 2 years since final discharge
Listed-crime history No conviction/adjudication for a listed crime in the prior 10 years
Pending listed-crime case No pending proceeding seeking such conviction/adjudication
Rehabilitation Rehabilitation attained to courts satisfaction
This route comes from 33 V.S.A. 5119(g). (

"https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/section/33/051/05119?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma rule: For anyone under 25 at offense date, check this path even if the offense would not fit the normal adult qualifying-crime list. Ask about rehabilitation evidence.

sourceSections.title: Juvenile sealing
sourceSections.text:
For delinquency matters adjudicated on or after July 1, 1996, Vermont generally seals the record after 2 years from final discharge unless the States Attorney objects and the court finds a listed-crime issue or lack of rehabilitation. For older delinquency matters, sealing is available by application or court motion if there is no later listed-crime conviction/adjudication or pending proceeding, and rehabilitation is shown. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/section/33/051/05119?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
For dismissed juvenile matters, if the person was charged with a criminal offense or was subject to a delinquency petition on or after July 1, 2006 and before age of majority, the court records are sealed immediately when the case is dismissed. Older dismissed juvenile cases may be sealed after 2 years from dismissal. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/section/33/051/05119?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
Step 1 - Get records
Wilma should tell the user to get both court records and a VCIC record check when possible. Vermont Judiciary says the person needs the case/docket number, year of offense, offense description, conviction date, and related details. Court records can be requested using Criminal Record Check Request form 200-00331, and VCIC provides statewide conviction reports for a fee. ("https://www.vtcourts.gov/criminal/expungement")
VCICs online criminal conviction record service currently lists a $30 cost per request, and charges are nonrefundable whether a record is found or not. ("https://secure.vermont.gov/DPS/criminalrecords/?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Step 2 - Determine the route
Use this order:
1 Was it a conviction or non-conviction?
2 If conviction, is the conduct no longer a crime?
3 If not, is it a qualifying misdemeanor, qualifying felony, qualifying DUI, 18-21 qualifying crime, under-25 offense, juvenile case, or unconditional pardon?
4 Are restitution and surcharges paid or waived?
5 Is there a pending charge?
6 Will the prosecutor stipulate?
Step 3 - Contact the prosecutor
The Vermont Judiciary recommends contacting the prosecutor, meaning the States Attorney or Attorney Generals Office, to ask if they will stipulate. If the prosecutor stipulates, the stipulation and petition can be filed, and the judge may review without a hearing. If the prosecutor will not stipulate, file the petition; the court notifies the prosecutor, the prosecutor notifies any victim, and a hearing is scheduled if the prosecutor opposes. ("https://www.vtcourts.gov/criminal/expungement")
Step 4 - File in the correct court
File in the Criminal Division of the Vermont Superior Court for the county/court that handled the case. If multiple offenses are from the same incident, one petition can cover them; unrelated offenses or offenses in multiple counties require separate petitions. ("https://www.vtcourts.gov/criminal/expungement")
Step 5 - Order and notice
If granted, the court sends the order to the person and notifies VCIC, the arresting agency, the Restitution Unit, and any other entity that may have a record. VCIC then gives notice to the FBIs National Crime Information Center. ("https://www.vtcourts.gov/criminal/expungement")

sourceSections.title: Fees
sourceSections.text:
There is generally no filing fee to ask to seal or expunge a Vermont criminal record. The exception is sealing a DUI conviction under 23 V.S.A. 1201(a) committed when the person was 25 or older; the Vermont Judiciary says there is a filing fee for that route, and a person can request waiver using Application to Waive Filing Fees and Service Costs - 600-00229. ("https://www.vtcourts.gov/criminal/expungement")
The statutory court-fee provision lists the DUI sealing filing fee as $90 and says that fee does not apply to other motions to seal or expunge criminal history under the listed record-clearance provisions. ("https://legislature.vermont.gov/statutes/section/32/017/01431?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

sourceSections.title: Wilma intake questions for Vermont
sourceSections.text:
Ask in this order:
1
Was this a Vermont case, federal case, or out-of-state case?

2
Was there a conviction, or was it dismissed, acquitted, no probable cause, or never charged?

3
What county and court handled it?

4
What is the docket number?

5
What was the exact offense and statute?

6
Were all charges from the same incident?

7
How old were you when the offense happened?

8
When did you complete every part of the sentence, including probation, supervision, jail, fines, restitution, and surcharges?

9
Do you owe restitution, surcharges, public defender fees, or court-ordered payments?

10
Do you have any pending criminal charges?

11
Have you had any later convictions?

12
Was the offense a DUI?

13
Do you have a commercial drivers license or permit?

14
Was the conviction for conduct that is no longer a crime, like certain cannabis conduct?

15
Was the offense a listed crime, sex offense, domestic/protective-order offense, vulnerable-adult offense, child offense, animal-cruelty offense, hate-motivated offense, or registry-related offense?

16
Would the prosecutor stipulate?

sourceSections.title: Waiting-period table
Agent-facing summary
sourceSections.text:
For Vermont, train Wilma with this core logic:
Vermont post-2025 = sealing-first, expungement-narrow.Expungement is mainly for convictions where the conduct is no longer criminal.Misdemeanor sealing = 3 years after completion, unless excluded.Felony sealing = 7 years after completion, but only listed felony categories.DUI sealing = 10 years, no CDL, special fee if offense committed at 25 or older.Non-convictions should seal within 60 days unless objection, and can be petitioned later.Age matters: 18-21 has a 30-day qualifying-crime route; under 25 has a broader 2-year rehabilitation route.Do not promise relief until Wilma has the docket, offense statute, disposition, sentence-completion date, payment status, pending-charge status, age at offense, and prosecutor-stipulation status.
